questions.q: What is the primary reason IPv6 was developed and how many addresses does IPv6 support compared to IPv4?
options: IPv6 was developed to address IPv4 address exhaustion; IPv6 supports 2^128 addresses versus IPv4's 2^32, providing vastly more address space
IPv6 was developed to improve routing speed; it supports 2^64 addresses, double that of IPv4
IPv6 eliminates the need for DHCP by assigning infinite addresses to each device
IPv6 only uses 32 more bits than IPv4, providing only marginally more addresses
questions.explanation: IPv4's 4 billion addresses were insufficient for the expanding internet. IPv6 uses 128 bits (2^128 ≈ 10^38 addresses), supporting virtually unlimited allocation. IPv6 still uses DHCP and DHCPv6; the address space increase is exponential, not marginal (2^96 times more).

questions.q: What is the correct structure and representation of an IPv6 address?
options: 128 bits divided into eight 16-bit hexadecimal groups separated by colons; consecutive zeros can be abbreviated with :: (used once per address)
128 bits in dotted decimal notation like IPv4, but with larger numbers separated by hyphens
A 32-bit number in binary format followed by a 96-bit subnet identifier
64 bits of network address and 64 bits of interface ID, always written as network/prefix
questions.explanation: IPv6 notation is: eight groups of four hex digits (e.g., 2001:0db8::8a2e:0370:7334), separated by colons. Leading zeros are omitted; consecutive all-zero groups compress to ::. IPv6 uses binary notation for subnetting, but addresses are written in hex. The 64/64 split is common but not mandatory.

questions.q: What are the main differences between IPv4 and IPv6 headers?
options: IPv6 simplified the header (removed checksum, fragmentation field, options) and added a 20-byte header versus IPv4's 20-60 bytes; IPv6 headers are fixed length unless extension headers are used
IPv6 added checksums at every hop for better error detection
IPv6 uses smaller 8-bit addresses to save header space
IPv6 integrated IPsec directly into the header, making it mandatory for all IPv6 packets
questions.explanation: IPv6 headers are always 40 bytes (fixed), removing IPv4 features like header checksum (delegated to upper layers) and in-band fragmentation (deferred to extension headers). This simplification enables faster processing. IPv6 does not add checksums or require IPsec; it supports optional extension headers for advanced features.

questions.q: What is stateless address autoconfiguration (SLAAC) and how does it differ from DHCPv6?
options: SLAAC uses Router Advertisements (RA) to allow hosts to self-generate addresses combining a network prefix with a host identifier; DHCPv6 provides centralized address and configuration management similar to DHCP in IPv4
SLAAC is deprecated in modern IPv6 deployments
Both SLAAC and DHCPv6 perform identical functions, and only one may be used per network
SLAAC requires manual configuration on every host
questions.explanation: SLAAC (RFC 4862) lets hosts learn the network prefix from RAs and generate their own 64-bit interface ID (often from MAC address or random). No server is needed. DHCPv6 provides centralized assignment and additional options (DNS, NTP). Networks can use SLAAC, DHCPv6, or both (DHCPv6 for extra options, SLAAC for addresses). SLAAC is still widely used.

questions.q: What is an IPv6 extension header and how does it differ from IPv4 options?
options: Extension headers allow IPv6 to defer advanced features (fragmentation, routing, encryption) to optional headers between main header and payload; they are processed only by specified nodes, not all routers, improving efficiency
Extension headers and IPv4 options are functionally identical
Extension headers must be processed by all routers along the path
IPv6 does not support extension headers, eliminating fragmentation entirely
questions.explanation: IPv6 extension headers (Next Header field chains them) include Hop-by-Hop, Routing, Fragment, Destination, Authentication, and Encapsulating Security Payload. Each specifies the next header type. By default, routers do not process destination options (only hosts), reducing router load. IPv4 options are simpler but less flexible. IPv6 still supports fragmentation via extension headers, not in-band.

questions.q: What is the difference between IPv4 and IPv6 regarding fragmentation?
options: IPv4 fragments packets at routers if needed; IPv6 delegates fragmentation to the sender (via ICMPv6 Path MTU Discovery) and routers must not fragment, dropping oversized packets instead
IPv6 never fragments packets under any circumstances
IPv4 does not support fragmentation
Fragmentation works identically in both; the difference is purely in naming
questions.explanation: IPv4 routers can fragment; IPv6 routers cannot. IPv6 senders learn the smallest MTU on a path (PMTUD) and avoid sending packets larger than that, preventing fragmentation. If a packet is too large, routers send ICMPv6 Packet Too Big messages. This shifts responsibility to the sender, improving router efficiency and reducing complexity.

questions.q: What are common IPv6 transition mechanisms and how do they bridge IPv4 and IPv6 networks?
options: Dual stack runs IPv4 and IPv6 simultaneously; tunneling encapsulates IPv6 in IPv4; NAT64 translates addresses between IPv6 clients and IPv4 servers; translation is necessary because IPv4 and IPv6 are incompatible at the network layer
Transition mechanisms allow routers to automatically convert IPv6 to IPv4 at runtime
IPv6 networks are fully backward compatible with IPv4, requiring no special mechanisms
The only transition mechanism is to turn off IPv4 everywhere simultaneously
questions.explanation: No single transition can replace both protocols overnight. Dual stack allows both to run; tunneling (6to4, Teredo) encapsulates IPv6 in IPv4 packets for routing over IPv4 infrastructure. NAT64 with DNS64 lets IPv6-only clients reach IPv4 servers. These mechanisms coexist during the gradual deployment phase. IPv4 and IPv6 are fundamentally incompatible.

questions.q: What is ICMPv6 and what role does it play in IPv6?
options: ICMPv6 is the IPv6 control and error messaging protocol, handling echo requests (ping), unreachable messages, path MTU discovery, neighbor discovery, and router advertisements
ICMPv6 is identical to ICMP in IPv4 with only superficial changes
ICMPv6 is optional and most IPv6 networks disable it for security
ICMPv6 replaces both ICMP and IGMP, consolidating their functions while remaining backward compatible with IPv4
questions.explanation: ICMPv6 is fundamental to IPv6, not optional. It includes ICMP functions (ping, error messages) plus Neighbor Discovery (replacing ARP), Router Discovery, and PMTUD. These are essential for IPv6 operation. ICMPv6 is IPv6-specific; IPv4 does not have access to it. Disabling ICMPv6 breaks core IPv6 functionality.

questions.q: What is the concept of IPv6 flow labels and how might they be used?
options: Flow labels are 20-bit identifiers in the IPv6 header that can mark packets belonging to the same flow, enabling routers to apply QoS or traffic classification without deep packet inspection
Flow labels are required identifiers that every IPv6 packet must set to a unique value
Flow labels replace the port numbers in transport layer protocols
Flow labels are mandatory for IPv6 packets to traverse the internet
questions.explanation: The IPv6 flow label (RFC 6437) lets sources tag related packets (e.g., a video stream) with the same label. Routers can thus identify and prioritize flows without parsing transport headers, improving QoS efficiency. Flow labels are optional (default 0); setting them is a sender choice. They do not replace ports or affect mandatory packet delivery.

questions.q: What is the relationship between IPv6 and multicast, and how does it differ from IPv4 multicast?
options: IPv6 integrates multicast as a fundamental feature (no broadcast addresses); addresses starting with ff00::/8 are multicast; neighbor discovery and router advertisements use multicast, making it more intrinsic than in IPv4 where multicast is often optional
IPv6 deprecates multicast entirely in favor of unicast
IPv4 has no multicast capability
IPv6 multicast addresses use the same ff00::/8 prefix but differ in scope encoding, which IPv4 also uses identically
questions.explanation: IPv6 removed broadcast (no ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff equivalent) and relies on multicast for link-local and network discovery. All-nodes multicast (ff02::1), all-routers (ff02::2), and solicited-node addresses (ff02::1:ffXX:XXXX) are essential. Scope bits (ff0S::) differentiate link-local, site-local, and global multicast. IPv4 uses 224.0.0.0/4 but does not mandate multicast for core functions.
